channel.title: Puzzle Games on Chasing Dings!
channel.description: Recent content in Puzzle Games on Chasing Dings!

item.title: Herdling
item.description: Care for and guide a growing herd of cattle-like Calicorn to a mysterious destination in this “Journey”-like wordless game of wonder and danger.

item.title: Blue Prince: Welcome to the Gift Shop
item.description: Hey, I completed another game! And what a journey…

item.title: Steam Next Fest: Day 1, the Weird Stuff
item.description: It’s the day when indies strut their stuff! Quick looks at Infinity Cats Project, Desktop Survivors 98, Inconceivable Rat Endeavor and Make Your Choice.

item.title: The Best Puzzle Games of 2024
item.description: These were the games that made me think this year.

item.title: Last Call BBS: Puzzle Games for the Retro-Inclined
item.description: The last game from the makers of TIS-100, SpaceChem, Exapunks and many other puzzle games feels like a Greatest Hits collection.

item.title: Superliminal: The return of GLaDOS
item.description: The snarky computer guiding you through Superliminal might not actually be Portal’s GLaDOS. but they at least went to the same school.

item.title: Star Trek Prodigy: Supernova
item.description:
Out of all the new-era Star Trek shows, Star Trek Prodigy is the one that strays furthest from the basic template. It’s the first targeted specifically toward children, and the first that, at least at first, doesn’t involve Starfleet.

It’s Star Trek for a generation that has been raised on the Disney Star Wars animated shows. And so it is likely the first Star Trek or Star Wars show to really ask – and answer – the question, who would win? Star Trek or Star Wars?

item.title: The 14 Pages of Sarah Woods
item.description: The Number 14: A Touching Game with Excellent Writing and Interesting Characters

item.title: EXAPUNKS Finished! Here's my solutions...
item.description:
Okay, I have been working on EXAPUNKS the last few weekends, and dreaming about it most nights. But I’m done with the main plot; got the achievement for proof, and I’m going to exorcise the game by writing down how I solved each puzzle.

First, some of my solutions are optimal, some are terrible, most fall somewhere in between. The first rule of programming is to get something working. Once you have an implementation, then you can go back and refactor; many times, your first pass is good enough. My boss used to tell me not to let the perfect be the enemy of the good; a good solution that produces the desired results is better than a perfect solution that is never finished. You’ll see what I mean by that when I get to some of my solutions.
